questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and nausea
A patient asking for a pillow
A patient wanting discharge paperwork
A patient asking for ice water

questions.rationale: New chest pressure with nausea may indicate acute cardiac ischemia and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Comfort requests are not the top priority. - C Discharge teaching can wait. - D Water is not urgent compared with possible cardiac symptoms.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Chest symptoms with nausea are high-priority until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Which action best helps reduce transmission when caring for a patient with a contagious infection?

options.text: Perform appropriate hand hygiene before and after care
Reuse gloves if they still look clean
Skip precautions if the patient feels better
Share equipment without cleaning it

questions.rationale: Hand hygiene remains one of the most effective infection prevention measures.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Simple infection-control steps still matter most.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse see first?

options.text: A patient with new chest pressure and diaphoresis
A patient needing help setting up a meal tray
A patient asking when rounds will begin
A patient who wants to know if family can visit later

questions.rationale: Chest pressure with diaphoresis can signal an acute cardiac emergency and must be assessed immediately. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Meal setup is not urgent. - C Rounds timing is nonurgent. - D Visiting questions can wait.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Acute chest symptoms outrank routine requests.

questions.stem: Which task is best for the nurse to delegate to AP?

options.text: Transporting a stable patient to radiology
Teaching a patient how to use an inhaler
Evaluating a new complaint of dizziness
Deciding whether chest pain needs an ECG

questions.rationale: Transporting a stable patient is a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse remains available for assessment follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing knowledge. - C Dizziness requires assessment first. - D Chest pain decisions require nursing judgment and escalation.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine tasks for stable patients, not assessment or teaching.

questions.stem: Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A patient with chest pain and diaphoresis
A patient asking for a blanket
A patient waiting for discharge teaching tomorrow
A patient requesting help finding a charger

questions.rationale: Chest pain with diaphoresis suggests a potentially unstable cardiovascular event and takes priority over routine comfort or nonurgent requests.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Unstable symptoms always come before comfort tasks.

questions.stem: A patient with schizophrenia becomes increasingly agitated in the day room, pacing, clenching fists, and stating loudly: 'Everyone is trying to poison me!' Which nurse action is the priority?

options.text: Approach the patient with two other staff members and prepare for physical restraint
Call security immediately and lock the day room
Calmly approach alone, make brief eye contact, use a low voice, and offer to move to a quieter space
Tell the patient loudly: 'You need to calm down or you will be restrained'

questions.rationale: De-escalation techniques include: calm tone of voice, non-threatening body language, offering choice/control (moving to quiet space), reducing environmental stimulation, and validating feelings without agreeing with delusions. Approaching with multiple staff (A) can feel threatening and escalate the situation. Locking the unit (B) removes the patient's sense of freedom and escalates. Threatening restraint (D) is coercive and contraindicated in de-escalation. Physical restraint is the last resort after verbal de-escalation fails.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: De-escalation: calm voice, non-threatening approach, reduce stimulation, offer choices — physical restraint is last resort only.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A charge nurse on a busy medical unit receives four calls simultaneously. Which situation should the charge nurse address PERSONALLY and FIRST?

options.text: A licensed practical nurse (LPN) reporting that a patient's scheduled morning medications have not been given
A CNA reporting that a patient pulled out their peripheral IV
A family member asking about the patient's discharge plan
A nurse reporting that a patient with chest pain and diaphoresis has an SpO2 of 88% and cannot be aroused

questions.rationale: The patient who is unresponsive, diaphoretic, hypoxic, and has chest pain is in acute cardiovascular or respiratory distress — this is a potential cardiac arrest or shock scenario requiring immediate, high-level nursing assessment and intervention. The charge nurse must respond in person and likely activate a rapid response or code. Missed scheduled medications is an important safety issue but not immediately life-threatening and can be delegated to the LPN's supervising RN. A dislodged IV requires reinsertion — important but not immediately life-threatening. Family discharge questions can be deferred to the primary nurse or case manager.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Triage for charge nurse response: unresponsive patient with chest pain + hypoxia + diaphoresis = potential code = charge nurse goes first and activates RRT/code response. All other issues can be triaged or delegated.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN who regularly works on a medical-surgical unit is floated to the cardiac ICU. Which action by the floating RN is most appropriate regarding scope of limitations?

options.text: Refuse the assignment because floating to a specialty unit is unsafe
Accept the assignment and manage all CICU patients including titrating vasopressors and managing IABP
Inform the charge nurse of any tasks outside her competence, accept patients within her skills, and request supervision for unfamiliar procedures
Accept the assignment only if she is designated as a second nurse, not as primary nurse

questions.rationale: Floating RNs are obligated to inform the charge nurse of limitations in specialty skills. They cannot refuse the assignment categorically (A) — refusing is appropriate only if the assignment is unsafe based on skills AND the unit cannot accommodate limitations. They must not perform tasks outside their competence independently (B). Accepting with disclosure of limitations and requesting supervision (C) is the professional and legally safe approach. The float nurse should receive a patient assignment matched to her competency level, with support for unfamiliar procedures. Simply being a 'second nurse' (D) does not adequately protect the patient or satisfy professional accountability.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Float nurses: disclose competency limitations to charge nurse, accept assignments within skills, request supervision — outright refusal is not appropriate unless assignment is unsafe and no accommodation is possible.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A neonate is assessed at 1 minute of life. The nurse notes: heart rate 98 bpm, weak cry, some flexion of extremities, grimace only in response to stimulation, and blue hands and feet with pink body. What is the APGAR score?
questions.rationale: APGAR scoring: Appearance (color): pink body, blue hands/feet (acrocyanosis) = 1. Pulse (HR): 98 bpm (<100) = 1. Grimace (reflex): grimace only = 1. Activity (muscle tone): some flexion = 1. Respirations: weak cry = 1. Total APGAR = 5. Score 7–10 = normal. Score 4–6 = moderate depression — requires oxygen and stimulation. Score 0–3 = severe depression requiring immediate resuscitation.

distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.c: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: APGAR ≤6 = requires intervention; acrocyanosis (blue hands/feet only) = 1 point; central cyanosis (blue trunk/face) = 0 points.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is with a patient who suddenly begins experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. What is the PRIORITY nursing action during the seizure?

options.text: Insert an oral airway to prevent tongue biting and aspiration
Restrain the patient's limbs to prevent injury
Protect the patient's head, clear the environment of hazards, turn the patient on their side, and time the seizure
Administer diazepam 10 mg IV immediately per standing orders

questions.rationale: During an active seizure, the nursing priorities are safety and observation: protect the head (soft padding), clear hazardous objects from the immediate environment, position on the side (lateral decubitus) to prevent aspiration when the seizure subsides, and time the duration. Nothing should be inserted into the mouth during an active seizure — this can cause injury to the patient and the nurse, and the jaw cannot be pried open safely. Physical restraint can cause musculoskeletal injury during tonic-clonic movements. Medication (benzodiazepine) is indicated for prolonged seizures (> 5 minutes, status epilepticus) — not every seizure automatically requires IV medication.

distractorRationales.a: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.b: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.
distractorRationales.d: This option is less safe because it delays the immediate nursing priority or does not match the highest-risk cue in the stem.

questions.takeaway: Active seizure: PROTECT (head, environment, airway position) → TIME → DO NOT insert objects in mouth, DO NOT restrain limbs. Post-seizure: lateral recovery position, assess airway, vitals, neuro. Administer benzodiazepines for status epilepticus (> 5 minutes).
